content.problemStatement:
In an undirected weighted graph, we define an s−ts - ts−t mincut cost as the minimum sum of weights of edges which have to be removed from the graph so that there would be no path between vertices sss and ttt.

You are given a two-dimensional array AAA with size N×NN \times NN×N. You are allowed to increase the value of each element of this array by any non-negative integer (these integers may be different for different elements). Afterwards, the array AAA should satisfy the following condition: there is a graph GGG with NNN vertices (numbered 111 through NNN) such that for each iii and jjj (1≤i,j≤N1 \le i, j \le N1≤i,j≤N), the i−ji-ji−j mincut cost in GGG is equal to AijA_{ij}Aij​.

Let's define the cost of the above operation as the sum of all integers added to elements of the initial array AAA. Compute the minimum possible cost.

**Note that: We consider only non-negative weight graphs.**
constraints: 0≤Aij≤1090 \le A_{ij} \le 10^90≤Aij​≤109 for each valid i,ji, ji,j
the sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 2,0002,0002,000
Subtasks**Subtask #1 (10 points):** Aij≤1A_{ij} \le 1Aij​≤1 for each valid i,ji, ji,j
content.inputFormat:
The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN.
NNN lines follow. For each iii (1≤i≤N1 \le i \le N1≤i≤N), the iii-th of these lines contains NNN space-separated integers Ai1,Ai2,…,AiNA_{i1}, A_{i2}, \dots, A_{iN}Ai1​,Ai2​,…,AiN​.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer — the minimum cost.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1: Of course, we must increase A12A_{12}A12​ by 111, since A21=1A_{21} = 1A21​=1. This is sufficient; one possible graph GGG is shown in the picture below. (Disconnecting vertices 111 and 222 costs 111.)

Example case 2: Similarly, we must increase A12A_{12}A12​ by 111, A31A_{31}A31​ by 111 and A32A_{32}A32​ also by 111. This is again sufficient and a possible graph GGG is shown in the picture below. (Disconnecting vertices 111 and 222 costs 111, disconnecting vertices 1,31, 31,3 costs 333 and disconnecting 2,32, 32,3 costs 111.)
